decks.name: Living and Non-living Things
decks.description: Learn the differences between living and non-living things around us

cards.back: Living things are those that can grow, breathe, eat, move, and reproduce. Examples include plants, animals, and humans.
cards.hint: Think about what you and a puppy can do that a rock cannot

cards.back: Non-living things do not grow, breathe, eat, or reproduce on their own. Examples include stones, water, and chairs.
cards.hint: Think about objects in your classroom that were never alive

cards.front: Name three things that all living things need to survive.
cards.back: Air, water, and food. All living things need these three things to stay alive and healthy.
cards.hint: What do you need every day to stay healthy?

cards.front: Is a seed living or non-living?
cards.back: A seed is a living thing! Even though it looks still, it can grow into a plant when it gets water, air, and sunlight.
cards.hint: Think about what happens when you plant a seed in soil

cards.front: What are the main characteristics of living things?
cards.back: Living things can grow, breathe, move, eat food, feel things, and produce young ones (reproduce).
cards.hint: Think of what makes a cat different from a toy cat

decks.name: Ecosystems
decks.description: Understand how living and non-living things work together in ecosystems

cards.back: An ecosystem is a place where living things (plants, animals) and non-living things (water, air, soil) live and work together.
cards.hint: Think of a pond with fish, plants, water, and sunlight all together

cards.back: A habitat is the natural home of a plant or animal where it finds food, water, and shelter. Examples include forests, deserts, and oceans.
cards.hint: Where does a fish live? Where does a monkey live?

cards.back: A food chain shows how energy passes from one living thing to another. For example: grass is eaten by a grasshopper, which is eaten by a frog, which is eaten by a snake.
cards.hint: Think about who eats whom in nature

cards.front: Why are plants important in an ecosystem?
cards.back: Plants make their own food using sunlight and give oxygen to other living things. They are called producers because they produce food for the ecosystem.
cards.hint: What do plants give us when they make their food?

cards.front: What happens if one part of an ecosystem is removed?
cards.back: If one part is removed, it affects all other parts. For example, if all the frogs disappear, insects will increase and the plants they eat will decrease.
cards.hint: Think of a chain - what happens if one link breaks?
